snippet: HbA1c target <7% for most non-pregnant adults. Metformin remains first-line unless contraindicated. Monitor eGFR annually and adjust medications accordingly.
SGLT-2 inhibitors or GLP-1 agonists preferred in patients with established cardiovascular disease or high CV risk. Lifestyle modification essential.
Random blood glucose ≥200 mg/dL with symptoms confirms diabetes. Fasting glucose ≥126 mg/dL on two occasions is diagnostic. HbA1c ≥6.5% is diagnostic.
Blood pressure target <130/80 mmHg for most adults. Initiate two medications if BP >20/10 mmHg above target. ACE inhibitors preferred in CKD and diabetes.
Lifestyle modification including salt restriction (<5g/day), weight loss, exercise, and smoking cessation recommended for all patients with hypertension.
Step-up therapy based on symptom control. Short-acting beta-agonists (SABA) for rescue. Inhaled corticosteroids (ICS) as controller therapy. Check inhaler technique at every visit.
Spirometry recommended for diagnosis. FEV1/FVC ratio <0.70 post-bronchodilator with reversibility confirms obstruction. Trigger identification essential.
Spirometry required for diagnosis: post-bronchodilator FEV1/FVC <0.7. Long-acting bronchodilators first-line. Pulmonary rehabilitation improves exercise tolerance.
Exacerbation risk assessment guides therapy. ICS added for frequent exacerbators with eosinophilia. Annual influenza and pneumococcal vaccination recommended.
Standard first-line therapy: 2HRZE/4HR (6-month regimen). DOTS (Directly Observed Treatment Short-course) recommended. Drug sensitivity testing mandatory before treatment.
Contacts of smear-positive TB cases should be screened. Isoniazid preventive therapy for LTBI in high-risk groups. HIV co-infection requires careful drug interaction management.
Severity classification: mild, moderate, severe, critical. Dexamethasone reduces mortality in severe/critical cases requiring supplemental oxygen. Anticoagulation for hospitalized patients.
SpO2 <94% requires supplemental oxygen. Prone positioning for severe ARDS. Remdesivir may reduce hospital duration in selected patients. Monitor for cytokine storm.
PSI/PORT score or CURB-65 for severity assessment and site-of-care decisions. Empiric antibiotics within 4 hours of presentation. Blood cultures before antibiotics if possible.
Outpatient CAP: amoxicillin or doxycycline. Inpatient non-ICU: beta-lactam + macrolide or respiratory fluoroquinolone. ICU: beta-lactam + azithromycin or fluoroquinolone.
Warning signs: abdominal pain, persistent vomiting, bleeding, lethargy, liver enlargement >2cm, rising hematocrit with rapid platelet fall. Require hospital admission.
NS1 antigen positive in first 5 days. IgM/IgG serology after day 5. IV fluid management critical—avoid overhydration. Platelet transfusion only for severe bleeding.
Azithromycin preferred for uncomplicated typhoid. Fluoroquinolones for severe cases but resistance increasing. Blood culture gold standard in first week of illness.
Iron deficiency anemia: ferritin <30 ng/mL, low MCV, low MCH. Oral iron (ferrous sulfate) 200mg TDS. Investigate underlying cause. IV iron if oral intolerant.
B12 deficiency anemia: macrocytic anemia, elevated MCV, neurological symptoms. Parenteral B12 (cyanocobalamin) 1000mcg IM monthly. Check intrinsic factor antibodies.
Levothyroxine is standard treatment. Start low in elderly and cardiac patients (25-50 mcg). Target TSH within normal range (0.5-4.0 mIU/L). Recheck TSH 6-8 weeks after dose change.
Methimazole preferred over propylthiouracil (except first trimester). Beta-blockers for symptom control. Radioactive iodine curative in most cases. Monitor thyroid function 4-6 weekly.
CKD staging based on eGFR and albuminuria. eGFR <60 for >3 months defines CKD. Nephrology referral at eGFR <30. Avoid NSAIDs. Control BP <130/80. Restrict protein.
ACE inhibitor or ARB recommended in CKD with proteinuria. Monitor potassium levels. Anemia management with ESA when Hb <10 g/dL. SGLT-2 inhibitors reduce CKD progression.
AKI defined as creatinine rise ≥0.3 mg/dL in 48h or ≥1.5× baseline in 7 days or urine output <0.5 mL/kg/h for 6h. Avoid nephrotoxins. Adequate hydration essential.
Child-Pugh and MELD scores guide prognosis. Ascites: sodium restriction, spironolactone, furosemide. SBP prophylaxis with norfloxacin. Avoid hepatotoxic drugs.
Asymptomatic gallstones generally managed conservatively. Cholecystectomy indicated for symptomatic gallstones, acute cholecystitis. Laparoscopic approach preferred.
Acute: triptans first-line for moderate-severe migraine. NSAIDs for mild. Preventive therapy if ≥4 headache days/month. Topiramate, propranolol, or CGRP antagonists.
Ischemic stroke: IV alteplase within 4.5h of onset if eligible. Mechanical thrombectomy within 24h for large vessel occlusion. Antiplatelet dual therapy (aspirin + clopidogrel) for 21 days.
Secondary prevention: anticoagulation for AF-related stroke. Statin therapy. BP control <130/80. Carotid endarterectomy if stenosis >70%. Rehabilitation early.
HFrEF (EF<40%): ACE inhibitor/ARB/ARNI + beta-blocker + MRA + SGLT-2 inhibitor = 'fantastic four'. Diuretics for fluid overload. ICD if EF<35% on optimal therapy.
Daily weight monitoring essential. Fluid restriction if Na<130. NYHA class guides therapy intensity. BNP/NT-proBNP guides diagnosis and monitoring of response.
Aspirin 75-100mg daily. High-intensity statin therapy. Beta-blocker post-MI. ACE inhibitor if EF reduced. PCI for symptomatic CAD with significant stenosis.
Antianginal therapy: beta-blockers or CCBs first-line. Nitrates for acute relief. Risk factor modification essential: BP <130/80, LDL <1.8 mmol/L, smoking cessation.
SSRI or SNRI first-line pharmacotherapy. Adequate trial ≥6-8 weeks at therapeutic dose. Combination with psychotherapy superior to either alone for moderate-severe depression.
CBT most effective psychological treatment. SSRIs/SNRIs first-line medications. Benzodiazepines only for short-term acute relief. Monitor for dependence.
CML: BCR-ABL1 tyrosine kinase inhibitors (imatinib, dasatinib) first-line. Monthly CBC monitoring. Regular PCR for BCR-ABL1 transcripts. Deep molecular response goal.
Hodgkin lymphoma: ABVD regimen standard. PET-CT for staging and response assessment. Non-Hodgkin lymphoma: R-CHOP for DLBCL. Bone marrow biopsy for staging.
Early aggressive treatment to prevent joint damage. Methotrexate first-line DMARD. Biologic DMARDs (TNF inhibitors) if inadequate MTX response. DAS28 score guides therapy.
Hydroxychloroquine recommended for all SLE unless contraindicated. Minimize glucocorticoid use. Belimumab or anifrolumab for refractory disease. Monitor renal function.
Ferritin <30 mcg/L diagnostic of iron deficiency. Ferritin 30-100 with transferrin saturation <20% suggests functional deficiency. Investigate GI blood loss in adults.
Causes: pernicious anemia, veganism, malabsorption. Symptoms: fatigue, glossitis, peripheral neuropathy, subacute combined degeneration of cord. Check anti-IF antibodies.
BMI ≥30 kg/m² is obese. Lifestyle intervention first: 500-750 kcal/day deficit, 150+ min moderate exercise/week. Pharmacotherapy if BMI ≥30 or ≥27 with comorbidities.
Uncomplicated UTI: nitrofurantoin or trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole 3-7 days. Fosfomycin single dose. Avoid fluoroquinolones for uncomplicated UTI. Urine culture if recurrent.
CT KUB gold standard for diagnosis. Stones <10mm: watchful waiting with hydration and alpha-blocker (tamsulosin). Stones >10mm: ureteroscopy or ESWL.
Appendicitis diagnosis: Alvarado score, CT abdomen with contrast. Laparoscopic appendectomy standard. Non-operative management with antibiotics in select uncomplicated cases.
Aggressive IV fluid resuscitation within 24 hours. Lactated Ringer's preferred. Pain management with IV opioids. Early oral feeding if tolerated. ERCP for biliary pancreatitis.
PPI therapy 8 weeks for erosive esophagitis. Lifestyle modification: head-of-bed elevation, avoid triggers. H2 blockers for maintenance. Endoscopy if alarm symptoms.
H. pylori eradication: triple therapy (PPI + amoxicillin + clarithromycin) 14 days. Test-and-treat for H. pylori. NSAIDs + PPI co-prescription in high-risk patients.
ANA, anti-dsDNA, complement levels for SLE. Rheumatoid factor, anti-CCP for RA. ANCA for vasculitis. Immunosuppressive therapy guided by disease severity and organ involvement.
Basal-bolus insulin regimen standard. Carbohydrate counting essential. Continuous glucose monitoring (CGM) improves glycemic control. Annual eye, foot, kidney screening.
Hypertensive urgency (BP >180/120 without target organ damage): oral antihypertensives, reduce BP by 25% in first hours. Avoid sudden drops causing ischemia.
CHA2DS2-VASc score for stroke risk. Anticoagulation if score ≥2 (male) or ≥3 (female). Rate control with beta-blockers or CCBs. Rhythm control with antiarrhythmics or ablation.
Wells score for PE probability. D-dimer if low probability. CTPA confirms diagnosis. Anticoagulation immediate if high suspicion. Thrombolysis for massive PE with hemodynamic compromise.
Compression ultrasound for diagnosis. Anticoagulation for 3-6 months minimum. Extended therapy if unprovoked or recurrent. DOACs preferred over warfarin for DVT/PE.
Hour-1 bundle: measure lactate, obtain blood cultures before antibiotics, administer broad-spectrum antibiotics, 30 mL/kg IV crystalloid if hypotension/lactate ≥4. Norepinephrine for MAP <65.
Empiric therapy: ceftriaxone + vancomycin ± ampicillin (elderly). Dexamethasone before/with first antibiotic dose for bacterial meningitis. LP before CT unless contraindicated.
Anti-seizure medications (ASM) based on seizure type. Valproate for generalized; carbamazepine/lamotrigine for focal. Medication withdrawal after 2 seizure-free years if EEG normal.
Levodopa most effective dopaminergic therapy. Dopamine agonists as initial therapy in younger patients. MAO-B inhibitors for mild symptoms. Refer for physical therapy early.
Cholinesterase inhibitors (donepezil, rivastigmine) for mild-moderate Alzheimer's. Memantine for moderate-severe. Non-pharmacological approaches for behavioral symptoms.
DEXA scan for T-score. T-score ≤-2.5 = osteoporosis. Bisphosphonates first-line. Calcium 1200mg + Vitamin D 800-1000 IU daily. FRAX score guides treatment threshold.
Acute flare: NSAIDs, colchicine, or glucocorticoids. ULT (allopurinol) when SUA >9 mg/dL or ≥2 flares/year. Target SUA <6 mg/dL (<5 mg/dL for tophaceous gout).
PASI and DLQI assess severity. Topical therapy for mild. Phototherapy for moderate. Biologics (TNF, IL-17, IL-23 inhibitors) for moderate-severe. Screen for psoriatic arthritis.
Crohn's: steroids for induction, azathioprine/6-MP for maintenance, biologics for refractory. UC: 5-ASA for mild-moderate; steroids, biologics, or JAK inhibitors for severe.
HBsAg positive + HBeAg positive + ALT elevated + HBV DNA >2000 IU/mL: initiate antiviral therapy. Tenofovir or entecavir first-line. Monitor for HCC every 6 months.
Direct-acting antivirals (DAA): sofosbuvir/velpatasvir or glecaprevir/pibrentasvir. 8-12 week regimens. Sustained virological response (SVR12) indicates cure. Test all patients born 1945-1965.
Antiretroviral therapy (ART) recommended for all HIV-positive persons regardless of CD4 count. Start within 7 days of diagnosis. Treat-to-undetectable. Monitor CD4 and viral load every 6 months.
Uncomplicated P. falciparum: artemisinin-based combination therapy (ACT). Severe malaria: IV artesunate. Blood smear or RDT for diagnosis. G6PD testing before primaquine.
High-intensity statin for ASCVD risk >7.5%. LDL target <70 mg/dL for very high risk, <100 mg/dL for high risk. Add ezetimibe if LDL not at goal on maximally tolerated statin.
Three of five criteria: waist ≥94cm (M)/80cm (F), TG ≥1.7 mmol/L, HDL<1.03 (M)/<1.29 (F), BP ≥130/85, FBG ≥5.6 mmol/L or DM. Lifestyle modification primary intervention.
Ultrasound for all thyroid nodules. TIRADS classification guides FNA indication. FNA for nodules ≥1cm with suspicious features. TSH suppressed: radionuclide scan first.
Annual mammogram recommended 45-54 years; biennial after 55. Women with BRCA1/2 or high lifetime risk: annual MRI + mammogram from age 30. BSE monthly from age 20.
Colonoscopy every 10 years or annual FIT testing starting age 45. Family history of CRC: colonoscopy at age 40 or 10 years before youngest affected relative.
Pap smear every 3 years (21-65). Co-testing Pap + HPV every 5 years (30-65). HPV vaccination recommended 9-26 years; consider up to 45. Post-colposcopy surveillance protocol.
PSA screening decision should be individualized (shared decision-making) starting age 40-55 for high risk. PSA >4 ng/mL: consider urology referral or MRI. PSAD assessment.
Non-pharmacological therapies first: PT, CBT, acupuncture. NSAIDs or acetaminophen before opioids. If opioids necessary, use lowest effective dose. Reassess regularly.

Exercise and weight loss core treatments. Topical NSAIDs preferred over oral for knee OA. Intra-articular steroids for acute flares. Refer for joint replacement when quality of life severely impacted.
Diagnostic criteria: widespread pain index ≥7 + symptom severity score ≥5 for ≥3 months. Treatment: low-dose amitriptyline, duloxetine, pregabalin, exercise, CBT.
AHI ≥5 with symptoms or ≥15 without symptoms. Polysomnography gold standard. CPAP therapy first-line for moderate-severe. Mandibular advancement device for mild. Weight loss.
TSH >2.5 mIU/L in first trimester warrants treatment. Levothyroxine dose increases 25-50% in pregnancy. Monitor TSH every 4 weeks in first trimester.
Diagnose with 75g OGTT at 24-28 weeks. Initial management: Medical Nutrition Therapy (MNT) and exercise. Insulin if glucose targets not met (FBG <95, 1h <140, 2h <120 mg/dL).
New-onset BP ≥140/90 after 20 weeks + proteinuria or severe features. MgSO4 for seizure prophylaxis. Antihypertensives for BP ≥160/110. Delivery based on gestational age and severity.
Hb <11 g/dL in first/third trimester, <10.5 in second trimester. Oral iron 60-120 mg elemental iron daily. IV iron if oral intolerant or malabsorption. Vitamin C enhances absorption.
Phototherapy based on age-specific bilirubin nomograms. Exchange transfusion for severe hyperbilirubinemia. Measure TSB or TcB in all jaundiced neonates before discharge.
Intermittent: SABA PRN. Mild persistent: low-dose ICS. Moderate persistent: medium-dose ICS or low-dose ICS + LABA. Severe: high-dose ICS + LABA + controller. Stepwise approach.
Amoxicillin first-line for CAP in fully immunized children >3 months. Azithromycin for suspected atypical. Hospitalization for severe: respiratory distress, SpO2 <90%.
DHF criteria: fever, thrombocytopenia ≤100,000, evidence of plasma leakage (rising hematocrit ≥20%, pleural effusion, ascites). Colloid IV fluids for severe plasma leakage.
NAFLD spectrum: steatosis → NASH → fibrosis → cirrhosis. Lifestyle: 5-10% weight loss. Vitamin E for NASH without diabetes. Semaglutide and obeticholic acid emerging treatments.
NSTEMI: dual antiplatelet (aspirin + P2Y12 inhibitor), anticoagulation, early invasive strategy within 24h for high-risk. STEMI: primary PCI within 90 min of medical contact.
COPD exacerbation: increased breathlessness, sputum, cough. Treat with SABA, systemic corticosteroids (40mg prednisolone 5 days), antibiotics if purulent sputum. Consider NIV.
Subclinical hypothyroidism: TSH 4.5-10 mIU/L with normal T4. Treat if symptomatic, TSH >10, pregnant, or planning pregnancy. Lower TSH target in elderly (up to 6 mIU/L acceptable).
Rotterdam criteria: 2 of 3: oligoovulation, clinical/biochemical hyperandrogenism, PCO morphology on US. OCP for menstrual irregularity. Metformin for IR/metabolic. Clomiphene for fertility.
Deficiency: 25(OH)D <20 ng/mL. Insufficiency: 20-29 ng/mL. Supplementation: 50,000 IU weekly x8 weeks then maintenance. Recheck level 3 months after supplementation.
ABI <0.90 confirms PAD. Antiplatelet therapy (aspirin or clopidogrel). Statin and ACE inhibitor. Supervised exercise program. Revascularization for severe limb ischemia or claudication.
Compression stockings (30-40 mmHg) cornerstone of treatment. Leg elevation. Exercise. Venotonics (e.g., diosmin) for symptom relief. Endovenous ablation for varicosities.
DMARDs (methotrexate) for mild-moderate. TNF inhibitors for active disease despite DMARDs. IL-17 or IL-23 inhibitors for psoriasis-predominant. Joint involvement guides biologic choice.

Hydroxyurea reduces painful crises and acute chest syndrome. Penicillin prophylaxis in children <5. Folic acid supplementation. Transcranial Doppler for stroke prevention. Bone marrow transplant curative.
Beta-thalassemia major: regular transfusions every 3-4 weeks, target Hb 9-10 g/dL pre-transfusion. Iron chelation mandatory (desferrioxamine or deferasirox). Monitor cardiac iron with MRI T2*.
Blood cultures x3 before antibiotics. Duke criteria for diagnosis. Empiric: vancomycin + gentamicin. Surgery for severe valvular regurgitation, heart failure, abscess, or persistent infection.
Small spontaneous pneumothorax (<2cm): observation. Large (≥2cm) or symptomatic: aspiration or chest drain. Tension pneumothorax: emergency needle decompression then chest drain.
